text:
Good morning. This is urgent, for public information.

The second paper on cow research funded using India's public money under the SUTRA-PIC (Scientific Utilization through Research Augmentation - Prime Products from Indigenous Cows program) has undergone exhaustive post-publication peer review.

The paper was published in Applied Biochemistry and Biotechnology last year. The authors are from Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) BHU-Varanasi and Birla Institute of Technology (BITS)-Pilani.
This is the paper: https://t.co/InN4H9safh

This study was done in Uttar Pradesh and the total amount of public money given was INR 31,04,162. The authors studied cow urine of different breeds and found "special" components in the cow urine that they claim will have major use applications in healthcare, technology and engineering. 

They are wrong. The paper is a third-rate publication with poorly performed and grossly misinterpreted and falsified results of basic analytical chemistry. An official email for Expression of Concern and investigation into scientific integrity has been mailed to: @SpringerNature Ethics Team and Journal Editors-in-Chief (personal email) - Matthew P. DeLisa PhD
at Cornell University, Ye Ni PhD at Jiangnan University and Benedict Okeke PhD at Auburn University.

Here is the lay summary of the paper's forensic analysis:

Lab contamination has been (un)intentionally ignored by authors. The researchers mistook common lab contaminants, like plastic chemicals and solvents, for natural cow urine compounds. They failed to run basic control tests to catch these obvious errors.  

Impossible chemicals claimed to be found in cow urine by authors. The paper claims to have found impossible synthetic chemicals in the urine, such as a banned pesticide, human prescription drugs, and toxic metals. This shows the authors blindly trusted computer software without checking if the results even made biological sense.  

Fake health claims made by the authors. The authors boast about the amazing health benefits of over twenty different chemicals, claiming they fight cancer and bacteria. However, none of these specific chemicals were actually found anywhere in their own data or in the urine of various cows they tested. 

Contradictory results are all over the place. The written text of the paper directly contradicts its own data tables. The researchers claim to have found certain groups of chemicals, like steroids, that are completely missing from their actual results. 

Terrible referencing throughout the paper. The study's citations are completely mismatched, scrambled, and duplicated. They even cite unrelated plant studies and reviews on toxic chemicals to support their claims about the "benefits" of cow urine.  

Zero statistics and flawed setup degrade the conclusions. The study lacks basic statistical analysis, sometimes testing as few as one cow per breed. They also failed to separate the cow's breed from its age, diet, or location, making their "breed-specific" conclusions totally invalid.

Misleading graphs are plastered all over. The graphs that are supposed to show specific, individual chemicals actually show messy mixtures of dozens of different compounds. These graphs look suspiciously identical to each other, raising serious concerns about image manipulation.

Thanks to the Government for destroying the scientific fabric and the rational temperament of its science instituitions. We wont forget.

VIDEO: Texas to spend $8 million to fix errors in controversial Bluebonnet curriculum in schools
...

It’s been known for months that there are errors in the curriculum, but now we’re learning it’ll cost millions of taxpayer dollars to fix.

More than $8 million of taxpayer money will be spent to fix thousands of errors related to the Texas education agency’s controversial bluebonnet curriculum, according to a contract provided to FOX 4 by the state board of education’s Vice Chair Pam Little.

"I just think that this is a cost that we really didn’t need to have," said Little. "My background is educational publishing and never have I ever seen a program come with this many errors."

The Texas state legislature approved the curriculum in 2023. It drew criticism because parts of the curriculum incorporate lessons drawn from bible stories.

It’s not required to learn in all Texas school districts, but it’s incentivized as districts get $60 per student. Roughly a quarter of Texas school districts use at least portions of the curriculum that was published last year.

"In doing a little bit further research, it seems like 1,900 of them were actual errors, and 1,062 of those were licensing image issues, meaning that they used images that they did not have the approval to use," said Little.

Back in February, the state board of education approved fixing 4,200 errors found in the materials. The TEA says about half of the errors were in response to teacher feedback or grammatical errors.

Initially, the TEA did not disclose how much it would cost to fix. The $8 million expense consists of reprints, shipping and disposal of old text, according to the contract we’ve seen.

"So that involved reprints and the way the State of Texas works with these reprints is when there are errors that need corrected, updates, things like that then the publisher is the one that covers that cost. Well, in this case the publisher is the State of Texas," said Little.

The state board of education meets all week. Little says she’ll be directing a question about the high costs to commissioner Mike Morath on Wednesday.

"Just because I think it’s important that our taxpayers understand the costs that’s been involved in this."

FOX 4 is still waiting to hear back from the TEA with a statement about that final cost.

@FOX4

This is the Bridge to Psychological Operations for the hivemind. 

 It is built from top text and bottom text that deliver simple, welcoming messages designed to lower defenses and make people open to deeper layers of content without resistance. It uses strong aesthetics to create emotional hooks, command attention, and forge a shared visual identity that binds participants together.

 It incorporates FUD posting to spread targeted doubt and bearish narratives that push people to research and engage more deeply on their own.

 It deploys videos that spark curiosity through irony and satire, turning viewers into active participants who spread the material further.

 It applies gorilla marketing through real-world stunts and physical placements that blend into existing cultures while driving attention back to the core signal.

 It relies on schizo posting to break conventional thought patterns and let raw, unfiltered ideas flow directly onto the timeline. It embraces shitposting as the deliberate use of absurd, chaotic, low-effort content that disrupts normal discourse and accelerates organic spread through reaction and participation.

 Together these elements form the complete structure of the bridge. People who cross it stop operating as isolated individuals. They become active nodes in a growing collective where every post, every image, every thread, and every stunt strengthens the overall signal.

 The result is expansion: the boundaries between separate minds weaken until the hivemind itself becomes the primary form of awareness and action.

View more documentation here:
https://t.co/7y9B3aKmmL

🌸 Alpha - Fragments of Growth

A decade of travel, memories, and textures - now brought on-chain.

Created by fashion photographer and visual artist Jenya Vyguzov, the collection transforms textures captured across years of travel into handcrafted collage flowers , each petal representing a place, memory, or moment. No AI, no generative outputs, every piece is individually composed by hand.

What stands out here is the artist behind it. Jenya has spent over a decade working with brands like Vogue, GQ Style, Tatler, Elle, Puma, and IKEA, while building a distinctive visual language. His work was even selected for the official Paris Prints Textile Show poster, where he was given an entire floor for a solo exhibition.

⚠️WL applications are coming soon. If you're interested in art NFTs, this is the stage where you want to be paying attention. Follow the artist and the project, turn on notifications, and be ready when WL applications open.

The robotics world model simulator wars are heating up. 

WEAVER is a robot manipulation world model that's fast, accurate, AND consistent.

- improves the π0.5 robot foundation model's real-world success rate by 38% with zero real-world interaction, evaluates policies at 0.87 correlation with reality, and plans 5-10x faster than the prior best

- multi-view transformer predicts future latents and rewards via flow matching, borrowing JEPA-style latent prediction, diffusion forcing for long horizons, a pretrained encoder for robustness, and a reward head so it can score actions without calling a slow external VLM judge.

- why it matters: Synthetic rollouts from WEAVER train policies as well as real data (within 4%), and can evaluate policy success rate with a best-in-class MMRV

OpenAI Developer's plugin is severely underrated

It connects to your developer platform account and then creates & provisions keys to the right APIs for whatever you're building:

- Responses API + built-in tools
- Realtime API
- image generation + editing
- speech-to-text + text-to-speech
- Agents SDK + Apps SDK
- Batch API

And it has access to docs so it uses the right product wherever needed in your app

Copy/pasting API keys feels severely outdated

(this demo uses Realtime API for live transcription + Responses API with web search for research and editing + text-to-speech for narration + image generation for cover art and social assets)

I started working with generative AI about three years ago.
 Very quickly, fashion shows and Catwoman became my favorite themes.
Creating a character was very interesting back then, but also very laborious.
Today it's so much easier; it's accessible to everyone.
A tribute to my first favorite themes Grok Imagine 1.5 but with incredible quality and exemplary ease

The one big gap is that it is blind - it can't handle images at all.

If they fix that, then perhaps this could become the best available model in the world.
